### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved five funding resolutions. The Public Health Department received a $102,464.72 grant increase from the National Institutes of Health for a training program on recruiting hard-to-reach populations for research studies. The Recreation and Park Department will receive $1.625 million in grants to design and improve Koshland Park. The District Attorney's office was approved to accept a $420,000 state grant to prosecute drunk and drug-impaired driving cases.

The Board also approved two infrastructure financing plans for development projects at Stonestown and at 3333/3700 California Street. These plans allow the city to use future tax revenue from these areas to fund infrastructure improvements needed for the projects. All resolutions passed unanimously.

### Summary:
The City Council passed two laws on December 18, 2025. The first requires community hiring and compensation standards for city-assisted housing projects, aiming to ensure fair wages and local job opportunities in new developments. The second law requires a study on how automated employment decision tools and algorithms affect workers, helping the city understand the impacts of automation on jobs. Both bills passed with overwhelming support (47 votes in favor), with only one council member voting against each.

### Summary:
The Transportation, Waterfront, and Seattle Center Committee recommended City Council approve an ordinance allowing HST Lessee West Seattle LLC to continue operating a pedestrian skybridge over Virginia Street in West Seattle for 15 years, with the option to renew for another 15 years. The committee also recommended passage of an ordinance to vacate an alley in the Denny Triangle neighborhood as part of a development project by GID Development Group in partnership with Seattle Parks and Recreation.

The committee received an informational update on Seattle's traffic safety efforts and progress toward Vision Zero goals, which aim to eliminate traffic deaths and serious injuries. No action was taken on this item.

### Summary:
The city is moving forward with several contracts and agreements set for committee review in May and June 2026. The Department of Transportation will spend $304,900 to install rapid flashing beacons (safety lights at intersections) and has authorized up to $1.5 million for electrical equipment purchases over five years. The Fire Department is approved to spend up to $2.5 million over five years on firefighter equipment from LN Curtis & Sons.

The city is also investing in workforce development and social services. The Economic and Workforce Development Department will spend $2.65 million in the first year (with $584,109 in additional amendments) to provide job training and employment services for adults, displaced workers, and youth. Additionally, the Police Department is partnering with USC's social work school to create internship opportunities for graduate students starting in August 2026, and the city will receive an update on the Community Police Review Agency's activities.

### Summary:
Denver approved nearly $3.6 million in equipment purchases to replace aging city vehicles and machinery. The city is buying a hydro-excavator ($522,883), two jet trucks ($506,413), two garbage trucks ($749,175), an asphalt paver ($551,045), a wheeled excavator ($638,880), and four stake-bed trucks ($506,100). The city also approved $729,247 for annual software renewal to keep computer security systems running.

In other business, the city amended a contract with Goodwill of Colorado to continue job training and support services for people on temporary assistance, allowing for employee retention bonuses and paid time off payouts. The city also added $200,000 to a flood control project with the Mile High Flood District for drainage improvements along the South Platte River, bringing that project's total to $8.2 million.

### Summary:
The council approved settlements and legal agreements for several lawsuits against the city. These include an $8,303.96 settlement with Novi Surgical Center related to a 2014 bus accident, and a $100,000 settlement with Debra Travis. The city also authorized legal representation for police officers and other city employees named in separate lawsuits.

The council voted to raise pay for several city jobs, including tree workers and auto repair staff. Tree Artisan Helpers will see wages increase from $9.96-$13.04 per hour to $11.00-$14.50, while Auto Repair Foremen salaries will jump from $45,700-$46,600 to $50,000-$56,200 annually. The council also reviewed a report on whether the city can collect revenue from professional sports ticket sales.

### Summary:
The City Council introduced several proposals focused on mental health and public safety. One bill would require schools to establish protocols for helping students experiencing mental health crises. Another would mandate mental health emergency response training for all police officers who regularly interact with arrested people and crime victims. A third proposal addresses pedestrian safety by updating crossing guidelines and right-of-way rules.

The Council also passed multiple resolutions asking the State Legislature to take action on healthcare issues, including increasing Medicaid reimbursement rates for nursing homes by at least 20 percent, expanding fertility treatment coverage, improving nursing home staffing, treating sickle cell disease, and tracking diabetes-related amputations in hospitals.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved a $350,000 settlement for a lawsuit involving cost overruns and delays on the Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital Building 5 project. The settlement resolves claims between the city and Build Group, Inc., and releases any related claims from subcontractors. In a separate vote, the board also approved the Police Department's drone inventory and usage policy, which complies with state law requirements.

Additionally, the board proclaimed September 2024 as San Francisco Transit Month and authorized the Police Department to produce a documentary called "SFPD Journey to Justice" about the connection between the Civil Rights Movement and modern policing. Public comment included requests for financial assistance from taxi medallion holders affected by ride-sharing competition and from the Special Police Officers Association seeking funding support.

### Summary:
The board approved new rules for compact mobile food operations, waiving license and permit fees for these vendors. The Public Works Department will now regulate compact mobile food operations and must consult with Public Health and Fire Department when creating rules for street vendors.

The board also approved several other measures: reallocating $34.4 million in the Police Department budget to cover projected overtime costs; requiring the Public Works Department to regularly evaluate and report on street sweeping performance; approving a police surveillance policy for electronic location tracking devices; extending the airport's contract with Hallmark Aviation Services for guest assistance; and extending agreements with the San Francisco AIDS Foundation and Rafiki Coalition for Health & Wellness to provide health access services through 2030.

### Summary:
The City Council approved a new law requiring training for local organizations on how the legislative process and parliamentary rules work.

The Council also approved three land-use items: leasing a vacant city-owned building at 349 East 140th Street in the Bronx to a community organization for 49 years to develop housing; designating the Barbey Building at 15-17 West 38th Street in Manhattan as a landmark; and designating the 29th Street Towers as a landmark.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed five housing development projects seeking Neighborhood Enterprise Zone Certificates, which provide tax incentives for new construction and rehabilitation. These projects include six new units at 64 Watson Avenue, four rehabilitated units at 449 W. Willis, 14 condominium units, 21 apartment units at 600 Woodward Avenue, and four new units at 284 Eliot Street. All five projects were recommended for approval by the City Planning Commission.

The committee also revisited two ongoing matters: the Board of Police Commissioners' expense reimbursement policy (clarifying that commissioners aren't paid but can be reimbursed for parking, mileage, and other reasonable expenses) and a status update on casino development funds from Detroit's three casinos, which each contributed $10 million for city business development. Additionally, a memorandum about Detroit Police Department salaries and benefits was submitted for committee review.

### Summary:
The council passed three laws affecting delivery workers and street vendors. One law improves safety rules for delivery workers using powered bicycles and third-party delivery services. Another law removes criminal penalties for general vendors and mobile food vendors—the mayor vetoed this, but the council overrode his veto and it became law anyway. A third law requires better protections and minimum payment standards for delivery workers, which the mayor also vetoed but the council again overrode.

The council voted 40 in favor, 6 against, with 1 member not voting and 3 absent. These changes aim to make conditions safer and fairer for people working in food delivery and street vending.

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council voted to approve a large batch of items including vendor payments, contracts, and equipment purchases for various city departments. Most notably, they approved a new ordinance allowing data centers to operate in the city under updated zoning rules, and they authorized payment for tactical equipment and technology for law enforcement.

The council also swore in five new members to various city commissions, including appointments to the Design Review Committee, Human Relations Commission, Human Services Commission, Mayor's Commission on Disability Issues, and Phoenix Aviation Advisory Board. A few specific agenda items were excluded from approval or were revised before the vote.

### Summary:
The city council approved changes to five downtown alley and street locations designated for "activation" activities. These include expanding the Jessie Alleys area to include the north side of Mission Street, expanding Minna Alley, Natoma Street, Second Street, and Yerba Buena Lane areas with specific street boundaries. The vote passed 10-0.

The council also approved a new police surveillance policy allowing the use of electronic location tracking devices, though one supervisor voted against it. In separate votes, the council approved new fees and definitions for mobile food operations (like food carts), waived certain permit fees for compact mobile food operations, and reallocated $34.3 million in the police budget from salaries to overtime costs. The council also accepted a $100,000 donation to Animal Care and Control.

### Summary:
The council approved changes to how police officers' secondary employment pay is set, shifting from fixed rates to allowing the Chief of Police to set rates (with council approval). The council also approved three community events: The Annex Group's "Home Opener Festival" on April 7 at 440 Madison Avenue, M-1 Rail's "QLINE Grand Opening" on May 12 at Bethune Street, and March of Dimes' "March for Babies Detroit" on April 29 at DMC-Brush Mall. All three events will include temporary street closures during their scheduled hours.

The council approved several city contracts totaling over $2.6 million, all fully funded by the city. These include demolition of three problem buildings ($74,400 combined), and new equipment purchases for Public Works: garbage truck loaders ($766,000), four cargo vans ($180,000), track loaders ($208,704), street sweepers ($952,000), and a street flusher ($224,669).

### Summary:
The committee discussed the sale of city surplus properties to develop auto repair businesses and related parking lots. One property at 8324/8348 Joy Road would be sold for $11,000, but the buyer must first obtain rezoning or a special permit since auto repair isn't currently allowed there, and must secure the property within six months. Two other vacant lots at 8326 Mt. Elliott and 8124 Michigan are already zoned for business use and can be converted to parking lots by right without additional approval.

The committee also reviewed exterior renovation requests for two downtown buildings—305 Michigan Ave. and One Griswold St.—which the Planning Commission recommended for approval with conditions. Additionally, the committee considered a zoning change for seven parcels on Frederick Avenue to allow mixed-use small-scale development in what is currently residential zoning, and received reports on the Detroit Land Bank Authority's third-quarter performance and a comparison of two competing community benefits proposals on the November ballot.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved several major spending commitments. They increased an Assessor-Recorder contract by $6.75 million (now totaling $33.9 million) and extended it through June 2032. They also approved a $75.9 million commitment to participate in a long-duration energy storage project running through 2050, increased airport security services funding by $10.5 million through 2028, and authorized a $94.6 million technology agreement with Mythics for Oracle products and cloud services through late 2028.

In other actions, the board passed an updated Climate Action Plan that revises the city's environmental goals and updates how city departments handle climate initiatives. They also accepted a $600,000 state grant to fund enforcement of labor laws and workers' rights through the City Attorney's office. A proposal to establish an Airport Surveillance Technology Policy for ride-sharing queue management was delayed for further consideration at the April 21 meeting.

### Summary:
The Committee on License and Consumer Protection recommended passing several ordinances that would loosen restrictions on alcohol and liquor licenses in Chicago. Specifically, the committee approved allowing additional package goods licenses (for off-premise alcohol sales like liquor stores) on portions of South Halsted Street, West Foster Avenue, and West Higgins Avenue. It also approved allowing both alcoholic liquor and package goods licenses on a portion of North Lincoln Avenue.

The committee also recommended passing an ordinance that would ban the issuance of any new retail tobacco dealer licenses going forward. Additionally, the committee approved changes to make it easier to license urban farms and community gardens, and to regulate produce merchants.

### Summary:
The City Council introduced several resolutions and proposed laws affecting New York City residents. They designated July 18 through August 17 as South Asian Heritage Month annually, called on state lawmakers to require comprehensive sexuality education in schools (grades K-12), and asked the Department of Education to work with faith organizations to teach religious diversity, improve hate crime reporting in schools, and protect students from bullying based on religion, clothing, or prayer needs. The council also pushed the state to legalize certain accessory dwelling units (small rental units on residential properties).

The council proposed three laws focused on public safety and city maintenance: requiring towing companies to clear debris from accident scenes, holding the city accountable for delays in repairing sidewalks damaged by city-owned trees, and requiring surveys before the city plants new trees. All items were referred to relevant committees for further consideration.

### Summary:
The city council discussed purchasing four downtown properties for $3.9 million to develop the Franklin Urban Sport and Entertainment (FUSE) Business District Project. The total cost includes $3.4 million for the land itself and $700,000 for demolition. The city will pay for this using its General Fund Reserve, which will drop from 31% to 27% after the purchase—still well above the state-required minimum of 8%.

Multiple community leaders and business owners spoke in support of the project, including representatives from the Chamber of Commerce, Downtown Gastonia Merchants Association, and Greater Gaston Development Corporation, who see it as a major opportunity for economic growth. One resident raised concerns about a nearby porta-potty company creating odors in the neighborhood and requested speed bumps on his street, which the city manager agreed to look into.

### Summary:
The City Council voted to approve three pieces of legislation. The first requires the Department of Education to report on manifestation determination reviews (a process related to student discipline). The second requires the police department to improve outreach about youth programs it offers. The third law mandates that the city regularly inspect and clean catch basins (storm drain systems) and report on that maintenance work. All three passed with 47 votes in favor, 1 against, and 3 council members absent.

Additionally, the Council advanced a proposal to create a city land bank, which is a program that manages vacant or underused city properties. The details of this proposal were not fully included in the meeting materials provided.

### Summary:
The council unanimously approved a neighborhood revitalization plan for the Marietta Street Corridor area. The plan includes job training programs, small business support, housing assistance, and sidewalk improvements. About 10% of the area has underdeveloped land available for mixed-use development, and the designation will help developers get extra points for federal funding.

The council also approved several routine items on the consent agenda, including a $709,900 contract for wastewater treatment plant equipment, acceptance of water and sewer extensions in new subdivisions, and $25,000 for a safe parking pilot program. Additionally, the council held a public hearing on a proposal to limit the Mayor's voting authority to tie-breaking votes only, though no vote was taken—the earliest vote will happen at the next council meeting.

### Summary:
The board approved a temporary closure of sidewalks on both sides of Howard Street between 3rd and 4th Streets from June 20-26, 2026, to allow the Config event sponsored by Figma to take place, pending a permit from the city's transportation office.

The board also considered several resolutions, including one affirming San Francisco's commitment to protecting civil liberties and privacy in the age of artificial intelligence, and supporting a state bill to fund food insecurity research and expand eligibility screening to households earning up to 400% of the federal poverty level. Additional resolutions called for protecting Medi-Cal dental funding for California children and supporting the Junior ROTC program in local schools.

### Summary:
The committee voted to recommend that the City Council pass a measure allowing Seattle to ask voters to approve a property tax increase for the library system. The tax would last up to seven years and pay for expanded library hours, updated collections and technology, programming, maintenance, and a seismic retrofit of one library building.

This directly affects Seattle residents' property taxes if voters approve it in the August 4, 2026 election. The measure aims to expand library access and services while making at least one facility safer and more resilient.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved several board appointments and reappointments. Anna Van Degna was reappointed to the Redevelopment Successor Agency Oversight Board through January 2030. Eric Dasmalchi and Timothy Kojo Minta were both appointed to the Treasure Island Development Authority Board, while Dominica Henderson and Stephanie Cajina were reappointed to the Municipal Transportation Agency Board. All votes passed 10-1.

The board also approved the Public Health Department's Services Act Annual Update for the 2025-2026 fiscal year. Additionally, the board recognized Molly Burke for 30 years of service at BART and La Casa de las Madres for their 50th anniversary of supporting the community.

### Summary:
# City Council Meeting Summary

The council approved amendments to contracts for Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport's Terminal 3 expansion project. The project will use union carpenters from Local 1912 for construction work, which council members said would bring quality skilled labor and economic benefits to the city through local hiring and job creation.

The council also approved lease and ground lease agreements related to airport operations, including a facility license agreement and an amendment with Cutter Aviation at Deer Valley Airport. Cutter Aviation is investing in new private hangars at Deer Valley, with plans to expand the facility significantly in coming years. During public comment, some residents urged the council to require higher wages (at least $25 per hour) for contract workers at the airports.

### Summary:
The city council approved several technology purchases totaling over $4.5 million, including a $949,972 contract with Davenport Group for server and storage systems, a $263,801 contract for a new backup system, and $511,327 for mobile laptop computers. The council also approved a $2.8 million accounting change to properly report software subscriptions in the city's financial statements, and a $8 million state grant through ARPA funds for water meter infrastructure upgrades.

The council held a public hearing on a proposed 191-townhome development on Dallas-Bessemer City Road. The project, which would annex about 32 acres into the city, includes one and two-bedroom units ranging from 1,500 to 1,700 square feet with at least two parking spaces each, plus over 100 guest parking spaces. The Planning Commission unanimously recommended approval of the zoning change needed for the project.

Ms. Crystal Certain, Finance Director, provided information concerning the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB) Statement No. 96 Subscription-Based Information Technology Arrangements. ITEM I: REGULAR AGENDA I.1: Public Hearing and Consideration of Adoption of Voluntary Annexation Ordinance – 32.404 Acres on Dallas-Bessemer City Road Presenter: Mr. L. Ashley Smith, City Attorney Mayor Reid opened Public Hearings I.1 and I.2 simultaneously. City Attorney Ash Smith explained Item I.1 is a voluntary annexation. He said staff has reviewed the request and believes that if annexed, City services could be provided to the property on the same terms and conditions as the current residents. Mr. Smith said this voluntary annexation accompanies a -303- Page 304 Page 304 REGULAR CITY COUNCIL MEETING – JUNE 20, 2023 rezoning and if Council does not wish to rezone the property, then the annexation should not be approved. (Reference as contained in Exhibit Book 9 to Minute Book 112 at Page 44) I.2: Public Hearing - Dallas Bessemer City Highway Townhomes (File #202200615) Subject hearing involves a request to assign a zoning district in conjunction with an annexation request for approximately 32.404 acres from Gaston County TMU (Transitional Mixed Use) and RLD (Residential Low Density) to City of Gastonia PD-RRDD (Planned Development - Revised Residential Development District). The subject property is located on Dallas Bessemer City Highway, north of the Gastonia Technology Park and is owned by Kenneth R. and Pamela S. Avery and Charles E. Pasour. Presenter: Mr. Jason Thompson, AICP, Planning Director Mr. Jason Thompson, AICP, Planning Director, explained this location is just to the west of Dallas Park and just to the north of the Gastonia Technology Park. He said the proposal is to approve a zoning to allow up to 191 townhomes. He said there are zoning conditions included in the agenda packet. Mr. Thompson said the Planning Commission voted unanimously to recommend approval. Mr. Thompson responded to questions. Mr. Kyle diPretoro, JIK Holdings, LLC, applicant, spoke about the project. A copy of Mr. diPretoro’s presentation is included in the exhibits. He explained that all of the units are alley-loaded. He said it is anticipated that the units will all be from 1500 to 1700 sq. feet and each unit will have at least two parking spaces. He said the plan includes over 100 guest parking spaces. Mr. diPretoro said the project will include a centralized green space. He said amenities will include a proposed natural walking trail that will comply with requirements for Dallas parks and will hopefully connect to the neighboring park. ### Summary:
Several residents spoke in favor of Faith Hope & Love Ministries and its work addressing homelessness in the community.

The city council approved multiple resolutions on a consent agenda, including $8 million in state grant funding for water and electric meter upgrades, $1.26 million for road resurfacing engineering, and about $2.7 million in technology equipment and server upgrades. Council also held public hearings on a voluntary annexation and zoning request for a new 191-townhome development on Dallas-Bessemer City Road, with units ranging from 1,500 to 1,700 square feet and guest parking spaces included.

### Summary:
The city council approved spending about $2 million on public works equipment, including dump trucks with log loaders ($1.3 million) and street sweepers ($707,800). The council also authorized contracts for a maternal health program consultant and a police technology coordinator, with a budget increase for the coordinator position.

Four properties facing demolition orders—at 12675 Riad, 9564 Beaverland, 3850-56 31st Street, and 20511 Gilchrist—have been given six-month deferrals because inspections found the buildings are secure and repairable. However, the property at 2437 S. Schaefer will proceed with demolition as originally ordered since it failed to meet deferral requirements and remains unmaintained. The council also discussed parking accommodations for delivery services in commercial areas.

### Summary:
The council approved two new laws affecting city streets and transportation. One requires planting vegetation on new medians that separate bike lanes from car traffic. The other directs the Department of Transportation to study the commuter van industry to better understand how it operates in the city.

The council also approved multiple zoning changes across the city. These included zoning map amendments in Manhattan and Brooklyn, changes to zoning rules, approval to acquire property in Brooklyn for public open space, and a special permit grant. These zoning decisions affect how land can be developed and used in those neighborhoods.

### Summary:
The city approved multiple demolition contracts totaling about $750,000 to tear down vacant and abandoned properties across various districts, with most funding coming from the city budget. These demolitions include 11 residential properties in District 5, 9 properties in Districts 1 and 7, 3 properties in District 3, emergency demolitions at 24 sites and one specific address, plus the demolition of Greenfield Park Elementary School.

The city also approved contracts for nutrition services and police department technology. Three organizations will provide WIC (Women, Infants, and Children) nutrition services through September 2017 using grant funding totaling about $1.9 million. The police department received approval for software maintenance for vehicle cameras ($118,000), a data security system for police vehicles ($208,000 in federal funding), and surveillance camera installation for the department.

### Summary:
The council approved multiple contracts totaling millions of dollars, all using city or state funding. Key contracts include: $13,800 and $20,500 for demolishing two problem properties; $263,546 for machine parts and cleaning services through 2019; $14.4 million to TMC Alliance for street lighting maintenance and support (a $2 million increase); and $170,000 to an accounting firm for the city's annual audit.

The council also expanded funding for three WIC (nutrition assistance) programs serving women, infants, and children, increasing their combined budgets by about $1.37 million through February 2017. All these contracts were referred to the Public Health and Safety Standing Committee for review on January 23, 2017.

### Summary:
A resident spoke against the rush hour reverse lanes on 7th Avenue and 7th Street, saying they're dangerous and don't follow the city's Complete Streets guidelines. She presented a petition signed by over 4,000 people and 50 businesses asking the city to remove the lanes and create a plan to educate drivers. Another resident submitted a petition about the city's golf courses, pointing out they use 1 billion gallons of drinking water annually and take up 20% of developed park land, and suggested creating a committee to study whether they're a good use of city resources.

The council approved several routine items including abandoning easements and rights-of-way at three locations, granting money to restore a historic building on W. Roosevelt Street, and changing zoning in two areas of District 6.

### Summary:
The council approved an encroachment agreement with the Rotary Club to install a Peace Pole at the Downtown Rotary Pavilion at no cost to the city. The pole will display "May Peace Prevail on Earth" in eight languages and is scheduled to be completed by November 8 when a visiting Rotary International official arrives.

The council also approved several routine budget matters, including $57,403 for elevator repairs at the Conference Center and $445,550 in insurance proceeds for storm damage repairs. They accepted a Recreation Advisory Commission report on summer facility use, fall sports, and capital improvements at three city parks, and voted to recognize October as National Colonial Heritage Month and September 15–October 15 as National Hispanic Heritage Month.

### Summary:
The Chicago Zoning Committee voted to recommend approval of zoning changes at 24 locations across the city. These changes allow property uses to shift—for example, from residential to commercial or mixed-use development. Most of the reclassifications were recommended to pass, affecting neighborhoods throughout Chicago including areas along Division Street, North Avenue, Halsted Street, Western Avenue, and others on the North and South sides.

Several zoning reclassification applications were discussed but no action was recorded yet, meaning they may require further review before a final council vote. These decisions will shape what kinds of buildings and businesses can be developed in these areas going forward.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors held a hearing on a housing project at 524-526 Vallejo Street and 4-4A San Antonio Place in Telegraph Hill-North Beach. The project would combine three dwelling units into one unit and restore a ground-floor unit in an existing four-unit building. Residents and housing advocates spoke both for and against the proposal during public comment.

The Board voted 10-1 to deny the project as proposed by the Planning Commission. A separate motion to override that denial and approve the project was tabled, meaning it was postponed and not voted on. The Board directed staff to prepare written findings for their decision, with a note that a three-unit project at the site might be considered if feasible.

### Summary:
The committee approved a request from Cintron World, Inc. to hold the "Cintron Pink Polo, Detroit" event at Palmer Park on July 15, 2017, running from noon to 8 p.m., with setup on July 14th. The Mayor's Office and all city departments recommended approval of the petition.

The committee also authorized the city to apply for an $87,500 grant from ArtPlace to restore five park parcels across Detroit by converting them to native grassland and installing interpretive signage. There is no matching fund requirement, and the General Services Department and Parks and Recreation Department are jointly requesting approval to submit the application.

Enforcement Records Search Platform And Related Services For The Oakland Police Department, At A Cost Not To Exceed One Million Twenty-Four Thousand Dollars ($1,024,000) For The Time Period July 1, 2026 To June 30, 2029; And (2) Waiving The Competitive Multiple Step Solicitation Process And The Local/Small Local Business Enterprise Requirements

### Summary:
The Oakland City Council approved a contract for a new enforcement records search platform for the Police Department. The system will cost up to $1,024,000 and run for three years, from July 2026 through June 2030. The council waived the standard competitive bidding process and local business requirements to move forward with this purchase.

### Summary:
The Committee on License and Consumer Protection recommended passing five separate ordinances that would allow new alcohol and liquor store licenses in specific Chicago neighborhoods. These changes would permit additional package goods licenses on W Fullerton Ave and W Irving Park Rd, and additional alcoholic liquor licenses on portions of W Belmont Ave and N Lincoln Ave.

The committee also recommended passing an ordinance that would restrict new alcohol and liquor store licenses on portions of N Lincoln Ave and various other streets across the city. All six ordinances were recommended to move forward for further city council consideration.

### Summary:
Council approved rezoning 385 South Zuni Street in Athmar Park from B-1 to E-MX-3. One speaker supported the change and no one opposed it. The vote passed.

Council also heard a second rezoning proposal for two properties in Gateway-Green Valley Ranch (17600 East 56th Avenue and 4991 North Telluride Street), which was postponed until June 8th. Upcoming public hearings are scheduled for April 13th on three separate rezoning proposals affecting multiple neighborhoods including Northeast Park Hill, Cherry Creek, and several south Denver areas. Residents who want to protest any of these proposals must file their objections by noon on April 6th.

### Summary:
The council is creating new rules to license and regulate commercial quadricycles (four-wheeled pedal vehicles) in Detroit. The proposal includes setting up licensing requirements for both business owners and operators, as well as updating the city code to clarify that passengers on these vehicles won't be charged with disorderly conduct. The items were referred to the Public Health and Safety Committee for a public hearing.

The council also approved a federal grant of $814,800 for port security (75 percent federally funded, 25 percent city match) running through August 2019. Additionally, four properties scheduled for demolition—at Goddard, W. Warren, Rutherford, and Wheeler—have been given a six-month deferral because inspections found the buildings are secure and potentially repairable. Two requests to close alleys and remove easements were recommended for denial by the city's Engineering Division.

### Summary:
Denver City Council approved three grants totaling over $2.6 million to support HIV/AIDS care and peer support services. The first grant of $2.1 million from the federal Department of Health and Human Services will fund treatment and support services for people living with HIV/AIDS through February 2027. The second grant of $506,000 from the Caring for Denver Foundation will establish a peer support hub to improve retention and job satisfaction among peer support workers, and will launch Denver's first peer support apprenticeship program by November 2027.

Council also approved over $4 million in contracts to repair and restore Denver's golf courses. The city expanded two existing contracts with Colorado Designscapes and Denver Concrete Works, adding $1.5 million each for concrete and landscape restoration work, extending those projects through October 2029. Additionally, a $1 million increase was approved for Colorado Designscapes for golf course restoration and repair services through October 2028. The council also designated a city-owned parcel near West 37th Avenue and North Jason Street as public right-of-way to officially establish it as part of the street system in Council District 1.

### Summary:
The council approved permits for multiple commercial signs at various locations around the city, including downtown addresses on Wacker Drive and Monroe Street, as well as neighborhood locations on Clark Street, Milwaukee Avenue, Broadway, and Ravenswood Avenue.

The council also passed several traffic and parking regulations, including a new Smart Streets Pilot Program that will test smart loading zones, updated transportation planning requirements, and various changes to parking rules, tow-away zones, loading zones, residential permit parking, traffic signals, and disabled parking spaces throughout the city.

### Summary:
The city council approved the 2025-26 property tax levy for Phoenix. The council also approved an amended contract with Keys to Change, a homeless services organization, that gives the city increased oversight. The amendments require a city representative to sit on Keys to Change's finance committee, participate in monthly meetings with leadership, and attend board meetings when needed. The city will review this arrangement in December 2025 to assess whether it's working and address any financial concerns.

The council took this action because of concerns about Keys to Change's management, including multi-year budget deficits and late payments to vendors. Keys to Change's leadership initially resisted having a city representative on the main board, arguing it could harm the organization's mission, but agreed to the compromise of having a representative on the finance committee instead. The city also approved several legal settlements and a proposed annexation in District 1.

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council approved previous meeting minutes from April, May, and March 2025. The council also approved several board and commission appointments, including Christoph Lohr to the Development Advisory Board, Kevin Mattingly to the Human Services Commission, Daniel Barajas to the Maryvale Village Planning Committee, and Janice Bradford to the Phoenix Business and Workforce Development Board.

The council voted on three liquor license applications. They recommended approval for Host International (District 8) and a bingo license for Greater Phoenix Deaf Senior Citizen (District 4). For KZ Beer & Smoke Shop (District 1), the council forwarded the application to the state without a recommendation, despite the applicant passing police inspection—this came after Vice Mayor O'Brien reported receiving a petition signed by 300 residents opposing the location. The council recommended denial of a liquor license for Ash Lounge (District 4) based on a recommendation from the Phoenix Police Department.

### Summary:
The city approved several routine items. A parcel of land in Council District 1 bounded by West 36th Avenue, North Zuni Street, West 37th Avenue, and North Alcott Street will be designated as a public alley. The city also approved a $700,000 purchase order with McCoy Sales Corp. to supply hoses, belts, and fittings for vehicle maintenance across all city fleet services through 2028, with possible renewal through 2030.

The council also moved forward with a new financial reporting requirement that will mandate quarterly financial reports be submitted to City Council. All items were approved by committee in early February and are scheduled for final council consideration by mid-March 2026.

CITY: Chicago DATE: 2022-12-12 BODY: Committee_on_License_and_Consumer_Protection AGENDA ITEM: Attendance AGENDA ITEM: Monthly Rule 45 Report AGENDA ITEM: Amendment of Municipal Code Section 4-60-023 (8.41(a)) to allow additional package goods licenses on portion(s) of S Stony Island Ave ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass. AGENDA ITEM: Designation of 45th Precinct of 19th Ward as Restricted Residential Zone prohibiting additional shared housing units and vacation rentals ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass. AGENDA ITEM: Amendment of Municipal Code Section 4-60-023 (40.50(c)) to allow additional package goods licenses on portion(s) of W Foster Ave AGENDA ITEM: One Time Exception to Wrigley Field Night Game Ordinance to allow for Chicago Cubs to hold Major League Baseball Game on July 14, 2023 ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass. AGENDA ITEM: Amendment of Municipal Code Section 4-60-023 (49.9) to disallow additional package goods licenses on portion(s) of W Morse Ave ACTION: The Ordinance was Recommended to Pass.

### Summary:
The committee recommended passing several changes to alcohol licensing rules. They approved allowing more package goods licenses (liquor stores) on portions of South Stony Island Avenue and West Foster Avenue, while blocking additional licenses on West Morse Avenue. They also designated the 45th Precinct in the 19th Ward as a restricted residential zone, which will prevent new shared housing units and vacation rentals from being added in that area.

The committee also recommended passing a one-time exception to allow the Chicago Cubs to play a night game at Wrigley Field on July 14, 2023, which would normally be prohibited under existing ordinances.

### Summary:
The council approved a rezoning of land near 23rd Avenue and Baseline Road in District 8 to support housing options for residents. They also authorized the city to apply for and accept a traffic signal optimization grant that will improve traffic flow, safety for pedestrians and cyclists, and reduce air pollution in Districts 7 and 8, particularly near the Broadway Curve area where residents have complained about timing issues.

The council also approved the acquisition of property at the southwest corner of W. Sahuaro and N. 28th Drives to build a new Fire Station 93 in District 1, and moved forward with a workforce training partnership amendment for construction services.

### Summary:
The Denver Health and Safety Committee approved an $18,000 increase to a contract with Clayton Early Learning, bringing the total to nearly $1.99 million. The additional funding will support behavioral services for students in Head Start and Early Head Start classrooms across council districts 3, 7, 8, and 11. The contract length remains unchanged.

The committee also received updates on Denver's clinical response system and heard an annual report from the Caring for Denver Foundation, though specific details on those items were not provided in the notes.

### Summary:
The council voted to approve most items on the agenda, with a few exceptions. Items 2-19 were recommended for approval, while items 20-60 were approved or adopted except for items 31, 38, 43, and 57-60. Item 56 was postponed to September 17, 2025.

A public hearing was held on two related zoning cases (items 58-59) involving a proposed 188-unit rental housing development on 14.37 acres in south Phoenix. The project would require rezoning to allow multi-family residential. The Planning Commission approved both items, but a community member appealed, citing concerns about traffic, emergency response times, and whether the project fit the area's character. The council postponed the final vote on both items to September 17, 2025, to allow more time for consideration.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors approved four resolutions with spending and leasing impacts. The Golden Gate National Parks Conservancy received an amended lease for Piers 31 and 33 to build a café and retail space over 30 years, with the city receiving $289,765 in back rent owed and a higher construction credit of $800,000. The Public Utilities Commission's three engineering contractors received contract increases totaling $21 million each (up to $63 million combined) for natural resources services through 2029.

The city also approved a 20-year lease with Twin Peaks Auto Care for their shop at 598 Portola Drive at an initial annual rent of $156,600 with 3% yearly increases. Additionally, the Positive Resource Center's emergency financial services contract was expanded by $5.4 million, bringing the total to $15.4 million and extending the agreement through February 2028 to help residents in financial crisis.

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council approved several board and commission appointments on November 19, 2025. Mayor Gallego recommended reappointments and new appointments to various city boards, including the Arts and Culture Commission, Development Advisory Board, Public Defender Review Committee, and others. The council voted to approve all recommended appointments.

The council also approved liquor licenses for 13 establishments across various districts, including restaurants, bars, and retail locations like 7-Eleven and AJ's. Two special event liquor licenses were also approved for St. Gabriel the Archangel Roman Catholic Parish and The Alliance Francaise of Greater Phoenix.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors voted to conditionally approve an AT&T wireless telecommunications facility at 350 Amber Drive. The project involves installing a 104-foot tall monopole with 12 new antennas and related equipment in a public zoning area. An appeal had been filed against the Planning Commission's original approval, but the Board moved forward with conditional approval after hearing public comment both in support and opposition to the project.

The conditional approval requires the project sponsor to meet additional safety requirements, including fire department inspection and compliance with fire codes for cellular antenna sites and diesel generators. The Board also required hiring a certified arborist to prepare a tree protection plan. These conditions were added to the original requirements set by the Planning Commission.

### Summary:
The board unanimously passed a resolution condemning Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for actions resulting in deaths. The measure calls for a third-party investigation into all ICE-related deaths, a pause on ICE detention until that investigation is complete and fixes are made, and reaffirms San Francisco's status as a sanctuary city.

The board also moved forward with reviewing 27 proposed historic landmark designations for buildings across San Francisco, including residential homes, churches, and a fire station. These proposals have been sent to the Land Use and Transportation Committee for further consideration before any final decisions are made.

### Summary:
The city is considering selling a vacant lot to a dental office for $4,500. The buyer plans to convert the property into a parking lot for customers and employees of their adjacent dental office. This use is allowed under the city's current zoning rules for the General Business district.

The proposed purchase agreement was referred to the Planning and Economic Development Committee for review on February 23, 2017. A separate agenda item about the status of properties on Tacoma between Anvil and Regent was also referred to the same committee.

### Summary:
The council approved several contracts and funding agreements. The city will spend $728,750 more (for a total of $2.75 million through January 2027) to administer federal disaster recovery funds through Base Tactical Disaster Recovery, Inc. The city also accepted a $6.6 million Community Development Block Grant from the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development to fund neighborhood and business development programs through 2033.

Additional approvals included a $4.6 million contract with ComplyAI, Inc. to implement an AI-powered building plan review system through 2031, and a one-year extension with Integral Consulting Inc. to continue soil and groundwater cleanup at the Denver Fire Training Center. The library received funding increases: $500,000 added to digitize the Rocky Mountain News collection for public access, and $960,090 added for online video streaming services through Kanopy.

### Summary:
The council approved the city's 2025-26 capital funds budget and the five-year Capital Improvement Program for 2025-30, which outline how the city will spend money on major projects and infrastructure.

The council also adopted numerous contracts and service agreements affecting city operations, including shelter services for families and seniors, legal services, software systems, and security improvements. One discussion centered on emergency shelter services, with a community member urging the city to reduce criminalization of minor violations, while Councilwoman Hernandez stated it was important to continue these services while developing better accountability approaches.

### Summary:
The New York City Council introduced several new bills for committee review. These include measures to regulate deceptive election content, clarify health code approval processes across multiple agencies, provide menstrual products in city bathrooms, register community gardens on privately-owned vacant lots, and rate city agencies on their hiring of minority and women-owned businesses.

Additional proposals would reduce penalties for restaurants that donate leftover food, update COVID-19 data reporting requirements, and adjust how civil penalties are calculated. All bills were referred to relevant committees for further discussion and review.

### Summary:
The city is selling four surplus properties to private buyers for redevelopment. A single-family home at 6077/6081 Avery will sell for $3,600 to someone planning to live there. A lot at 6081 Avery will sell for $4,990 and be converted to green space. A Commonwealth Avenue property will sell for $23,487 and be demolished to make way for a laundry facility. An auto repair shop will buy parking space at Gratiot Avenue for $4,008 and must demolish the existing building within a year. Additionally, the city is rescinding a 2001 agreement with Greater Corktown Development Corporation regarding property on Bagley Avenue that was supposed to have two homes built on it.

The committee also scheduled three public hearings on tax incentive zones. These include a Neighborhood Enterprise Zone near the old Tiger Stadium area, an Obsolete Property Rehabilitation District on East Grand Boulevard, and a Commercial Rehabilitation District near Van Dyke Street. These designations typically offer tax breaks to encourage development and investment in those areas.

### Summary:
The council approved an expanded "Know Your Rights" webpage that will include information about healthcare facilities, social services, domestic violence survivor services, and emergency relief resources. The city will also hold quarterly community listening sessions to update residents on how these resources are being implemented, with at least four meetings scheduled during the first year.

The council also swapped several administrative items, approving most agenda items (Items 17-80) while holding back a few for further review or later dates. Several new members were sworn in to city committees, including positions on the Audit Committee, Environmental Quality Commission, and Youth and Education Commission. The council also voted to authorize the city controller to disburse funds to pay vendors, contractors, and maintain existing city contracts.

### Summary:
The city approved several purchasing and service agreements. The Denver Fire Department will buy turnout gear for $5.3 million through March 2028, and the city fleet will purchase auto parts from Genuine Parts Company for $4 million through January 2029. The city also approved a 10-year food and beverage concession at Pahaska in Mountain Parks with an indigenous-focused vendor, and authorized Skyline Park Block 1 in downtown Denver to be used for year-round markets and public events.

Additionally, the city will continue its program of donating surplus bison to American Indian tribes and nonprofits, and approved an agreement with the Denver Mountain Parks Foundation to operate Mount Blue Sky Recreation Area through the end of 2029. The council also approved changes to the employee classification and pay plan for city workers in the Career Service.

### Summary:
The committee approved several public events for 2017, including CBS Radio's "97.1 The Ticket Salutes the Tigers" at Grand Circus Park on April 7, the Susan G. Komen Race for the Cure at Comerica Park on May 13, and a health run at Joy Road and Spinoza on July 22. All three events have temporary street closures and received support from the Mayor's Office and city departments.

The committee also approved a five-year Parks and Recreation Plan that will help the city pursue grant funding to improve parks and recreation centers across Detroit. Additionally, the Eastside Tennis Club's operating contract at 18201 E. Warren Avenue was extended for another five years through October 2022, with a total contract value of $138,876.

### Summary:
The City Council referred four land use applications to committee for review. The first involves leasing an unused city-owned parcel at 349 East 140th Street in the Bronx to a community land trust for 49 years (with renewal options) to develop housing. The other three applications all relate to a project at 1640 Anthony Avenue in the Bronx and include: designating the area for urban development, changing the zoning from R7-1 to R8 to allow denser building, and establishing mandatory affordable housing requirements for new development at that location.

All applications were sponsored by Council Member Salamanca Jr. and have been sent to the Committee on Land Use and the Subcommittee on Landmarks, Public Sitings and Dispositions for further review and public hearings.

CITY: Denver DATE: 2026-03-24 BODY: Parks,_Art_and_Culture AGENDA ITEM: Consent Items AGENDA ITEM: A resolution approving a proposed Amendatory Agreement between the City and County of Denver and Public Service Company of Colorado to continue a non-exclusive sponsorship agreement. Amends a contract with Public Service Company of Colorado to add $386,363.00 for a new total of $600,413.00 and to add three years for a new end date of 3-31-2029 to continue a non-exclusive sponsorship agreement, Council District 10 and Mountain Parks (THTRS-202473245/THTRS-202582910-01). The last regularly scheduled Council meeting within the 30-day review period is on 4-27-2026. The Committee approved filing this item at its meeting on 3-24-2026. ### Summary:
The Parks, Art and Culture Committee approved an amended sponsorship agreement with Public Service Company of Colorado. The city is adding $386,363 to the existing contract, bringing the total to $600,413, and extending the agreement by three years through March 31, 2029.

The resolution was approved by consent, meaning it passed without debate. The sponsorship supports parks and cultural programs in Council District 10 and Mountain Parks.

She talked about the temperatures workers faced working on the tarmac.\par \par Councilwoman Guardado inquired what airline Ms. David worked for.\par \par Ms. David replied she worked with American Airlines.\par \par Councilwoman Guardado asked for confirmation the heat gun Ms. David mentioned had registered 155 degrees on the tarmac.\par \par Ms. David answered that was correct.\par \par Councilwoman Guardado questioned how accessible water would be for Ms. David when temperatures rise.\par \par Ms. David responded most of her coworkers carried water bottles with them, and talked about the company coworkers relied on to come by with water.\par \par Councilwoman Guardado asked if Ms. David was able to look at the heat plan provided by her employer.\par \par Ms. David replied she did, and commented the ordinance passed last year didn't change anything.\par \par Councilwoman Guardado inquired if Ms. Davis had access to a cool place if the temperatures were too hot for her.\par \par Ms. David answered tents on the ramp were provided, and talked about the differences between different jobs at the airport and the type of cooling provided.\par \par Cecilia Ortiz mentioned she was a warehouse employee at the airport, and was the City's Contract Worker on the Heat Safety Advisory Committee. She expressed appreciation for everyone's leadership on this issue, and \b requested an amendment to her motion to adopt Item 47 with staff direction that as the City moves forward with the reforms presented today, that this topic is brought back to Council in the Fall to talk about reforms brought forward by workers.\par \par Councilwoman Pastor agreed to the amendment.\par \par \b0 Councilwoman Guardado thanked councilmembers, and talked about her concerns with retaliation among employers at the airport. She commented on Sky Harbor Airport being the friendliest airport in the country, and made suggestions to improve conditions for workers at the airport.\par \par Mayor Gallego thanked Councilwoman Guardado for her work on this, and questioned if heat regulations were different for City workers rather than City business partners.\par \par Assistant City Manager Lori Bays replied that was correct, and noted heat regulations for City employees were administrative regulations. She cited Administrative Regulation 2.31B that address

### Summary:
The council discussed improvements to a heat safety ordinance for workers at Sky Harbor Airport that was passed last year. Multiple workers testified about dangerous conditions, including tarmac temperatures reaching 155 degrees, and said the existing ordinance hasn't adequately protected them. Some workers reported their employers weren't complying with air conditioning requirements for vehicles by the May 1, 2025 deadline, and one airport warehouse employee said she was fired after the ordinance passed.

Council members directed city staff to investigate non-compliant vehicles and ordered the airport to require all company vehicles on the tarmac to have working air conditioning. The council voted to adopt updated protections and requested that this issue be brought back for review in the fall to discuss further reforms. Councilwoman Guardado also raised concerns about worker retaliation and the gap between heat protections for city employees versus contractors.

### Summary:
Council approved changes to allow outdoor dining on city streets, with new rules added to the municipal code to govern how restaurants can use sidewalk and street space. They also approved spending opioid settlement funds and allocated money to support recently arrived migrants in the city.

Council also passed a budget adjustment affecting various city departments including the Police and Fire committees, Health and Human Relations, and workforce development. The council spent time recognizing local community members, organizations, and church leaders for their contributions and anniversaries.

### Summary:
The council approved signage permits for 16 different locations across the city, including multiple signs at 325 N Wells St and individual permits at addresses like 625 S Ashland Ave, 3333 W Belmont Ave, and other sites throughout Chicago. All permits passed after motions by Alderman Ramirez-Rosa, with unsuccessful attempts to reconsider the votes.

The council also passed resolutions congratulating Kenwood Academy High School for winning the 2023 Chicago Public League City Baseball Championship and officially recognizing May 2023 as Jewish American Heritage Month.

### Summary:
The council approved a rezoning change near 27th Avenue and Baseline Road in District 8, and adopted the Historic Preservation Plan for the city, which provides guidelines for protecting historic properties throughout Phoenix. No one from the public spoke during the hearings on either item.

The council also approved a water service line replacement project citywide and advanced a rezoning application for the Metro North Corporate Park near Thunderbird Road and Black Canyon Highway in District 1. A separate rezoning request near 32nd Street and Sunnyside Lane in District 3 was withdrawn, and another rezoning application near 99th Avenue and Thomas Road was postponed to December 17.

### Summary:
The committee discussed several ongoing issues including burned-out homes on Sussex and Kendall streets, an abandoned home at 14034 Southfield Road, and requests to address blight in the city's viaducts. Members also reviewed proposals for a senior discount on trash collection fees and questions about drainage fees. Two special events were approved: a Shinola Audio Launch Event on November 21 and the Cupid's Undie Run on February 11, 2017, both with temporary street closures.

The committee also moved forward on several city contracts totaling millions of dollars. These include an $8.7 million contract for parking management and abandoned vehicle operations through 2020, $1.3 million for new firefighter gear, $273,000 for police license plate readers and car cameras, and $200,000 for police technical assistance from Wayne State University. Additional contracts were approved for WIC and immunization services office space and hazmat assessment for commercial demolitions.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors voted to officially designate the Rainbow Flag Installation at Harvey Milk Plaza as a San Francisco landmark and passed an ordinance banning landlords from using automated software or algorithms to set rents or manage occupancy in residential units. Both measures passed on their first reading with strong support.

The board also adopted resolutions recognizing July 2024 as Disability Pride Month and declaring August 4-10 as National Health Center Week in the city. Additionally, supervisors voted to waive permit fees for the Vicha Ratanapakdee Mosaic Stairway Project.

Mayor Franks strongly objected to the proposal, stating that removing his vote would diminish representation for citizens. He argued that the change could create situations where just three Council members (not a majority of the full Council) could effectively block initiatives. He emphasized that the City had always allowed Mayors to vote and this was part of Gastonia's tradition, suggesting that if changes were to be made, they should be put before voters. Councilwoman Stepp raised concerns about accountability and transparency, suggesting that voters should know where elected officials stand on issues through their voting record. She questioned why the change was being proposed now when the current system had worked for decades. Mayor Pro Tem Gallagher explained that after surveying municipalities throughout North Carolina, it was found that only two cities (Gastonia and Newton) where the Mayor votes regularly. He suggested that it would make more sense for the Mayor to concentrate on running the meetings without worrying about when to vote. Mayor Pro Tem Gallagher said the Mayor would have the most leverage if voting to break a tie. City Attorney Eric Edgerton provided legal clarification, explaining that under state statute (160A- 101), cities have two options: the Mayor can either vote on all matters or only in case of a tie. He noted that Gastonia's current system (where the Mayor chooses when to vote) appeared to be a hybrid approach that had been in place since at least 1971. Councilwoman Barber supported the proposal, noting that as a presider and chair of a meeting, the Mayor's main focus should be on the process of the meeting. She pointed out that many residents were unaware the Mayor could vote, as previous Mayors often chose not to vote and focused solely on presiding over meetings. -216- Page 217 Page 217 REGULAR CITY COUNCIL MEETING – FEBRUARY 3, 2026 Councilman Silverman suggested the current governing statute and its language be included in the public-facing portion of the agenda. Following discussion, it was the consensus of Council that the matter would be brought back for a vote at the next Council meeting, as required by statute. City Attorney Edgerton will prepare additional information about the relevant statutes to be included on the agenda and current practices in other municipalities. Mayor Pro Tem Gallagher made a motion to close the Public Hearing. Councilwoman Barber seconded the motion and it was approved unanimously. ### Summary:
The council discussed a proposal to change how the Mayor votes on city matters. Currently, the Mayor can choose when to vote, but the proposal would limit voting to tie-breaking situations only. Mayor Franks opposed the change, saying it would reduce citizen representation, while supporters like Councilwoman Barber argued the Mayor should focus on running meetings rather than voting. The City Attorney noted that state law allows cities to choose between two options: the Mayor votes on everything or only on ties. A vote on this proposal will happen at the next council meeting.

In other business, the City Manager praised city staff for their response to recent winter storms across all departments. The council also discussed upcoming strategic planning goals and objectives that will be presented at the next meeting. Several council members thanked staff for their storm response and promoted community volunteer opportunities, including a cleanup event on March 14 and volunteering at a warming center for people experiencing homelessness.

### Summary:
The City Council approved zoning changes for the Western Rail Yards development in Manhattan's Hudson Yards neighborhood. The council modified special permit rules for the Hudson Yards District and changed street grades on West 33rd Street between Eleventh and Twelfth Avenues. The changes also created a public access easement as part of the project.

All three related resolutions were approved by the council in June 2025 after going through the zoning and land use review process. The modifications were recommended by the City Planning Commission.

### Summary:
The city council approved three spending agreements. First, they approved a $5.3 million contract with MES Service Company to buy protective turnout gear for firefighters through March 2028, with the option to renew. Second, they approved a $4 million contract with Genuine Parts Company (NAPPA Auto Parts) to supply replacement parts for city vehicles through January 2029, also with renewal options.

The council also increased funding for the WorkReady Denver program by $300,000, bringing the total contract with Centro Humanitario Para Los Trabajadores to $2.55 million. This program helps people who are working toward legal authorization to get job training and placement in fields that have worker shortages.

### Summary:
The police department is reinstating ICAT training for all officers by the end of 2025, with additional training on tactical positioning (time, distance, and cover) completed by October 2025. Chief Giordano met with precinct commanders to set expectations and emphasized keeping communication channels open. When questioned about whether retraining would be effective since officers had received ICAT training before, the Chief said the department is committed to ongoing training improvements and will continue looking for additional training opportunities.

The city council approved a contract with Benchmark Analytics for an Early Intervention System that will monitor officer behavior to identify potential problems early. The system will track 91 different variables including calls for service, citations, and stops—going beyond just internal affairs data. Supervisors will have conversations with officers flagged by the system and set up intervention plans if needed, which could include training or wellness resources rather than immediate discipline. The system is expected to take 12-18 months to fully integrate with police databases once the contract is signed, and results will be made public.

### Summary:
The city council approved several routine resolutions on the consent agenda, including $57,403 for elevator repairs at the Conference Center, $445,550 in insurance proceeds for storm damage repairs, and acceptance of a $500,000 federal grant for economic development. The council also approved a lease agreement with Gaston College to build a law enforcement de-escalation training facility on city property.

In a separate vote, the council approved changes to sign regulations in downtown Gastonia. The amendment allows roof signs and a new type called "skyline signs" in the Central Business District, expanding where these signs can be placed.

### Summary:
The council unanimously approved the annexation and rezoning of approximately 219 acres on Union New Hope Road for single-family residential development. They also approved a $60,000 annual contract with the Gaston Business Association to manage the city's Municipal Services District, with funds coming from the Municipal Services District budget.

In other business, the council approved a 15-year lease agreement for about 2,984 square feet of CaroMont Health Park stadium to Zawyer Sports and Entertainment Group, with possession required by March 15. The council also approved spending $4.86 million from general and solid waste funds to purchase vehicles and equipment for the Fire Department, Police Department, and Solid Waste Division.

### Summary:
The City Council unanimously passed three resolutions. One establishes July 26 as Haitian Konpa Day each year to celebrate Haitian music and dance. Another calls on the New York State Legislature to require landlords give Mitchell-Lama residents at least 60 days' notice before raising their rent. A third designates November 12 as Sigma Gamma Rho Day to recognize the sorority's community service work.

The Council also introduced three new bills for future consideration. One would create overnight parking spaces for electric and low-emission delivery vehicles in industrial areas. Another would establish resources and support for doulas (birth coaches). A third would require public education about traumatic brain injuries and concussions. These bills were referred to relevant committees for review before any vote.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed four event petitions, all recommended for approval by the Mayor's Office and city departments. These include: a Senior Citizen Info Summit at Erma Henderson Park on July 20; a health run called "STAY Fit" on July 22 with temporary street closures at Joy Road and Spinoza; the Palmer Park Art Fair on May 6-7; and a Red Bull event at Hart Plaza on May 13-14. All events involve temporary street closures and setup/teardown periods.

The committee also discussed two ongoing requests: an update on the Entertainment Commission status and a parking situation at Comstock Park that has been pending since November 2016.

### Summary:
The board approved an amendment to the Mission Bay South Redevelopment Plan that will allow a taller affordable housing building on Block 4 East. The changes increase the maximum building height from 160 feet to 250 feet and permit more housing units on the northern half of the block. The measure passed on first reading with all 11 supervisors voting yes.

The meeting also recognized several community contributors with certificates of honor, including volunteers from Alta Plaza Park, a Cantonese language professor from City College, and a retired city planning department manager with over 20 years of service. A posthumous award was presented to Richard Pena for his cultural contributions to the Mission District.

### Summary:
The council approved three federally-funded contracts totaling $400,000 to provide emergency shelter and transitional housing for Detroit residents through local organizations: Cass Community Social Services ($100,000), St. John Community Center ($100,000), and Southwest Counseling Solutions ($200,000). These contracts run through June 30, 2018.

The council also approved several development projects, including a nine-unit townhome and carriage-home development in Brush Park and a mixed-use development on Mack Avenue. One zoning change request to allow business use in a residential area was recommended for denial. Additionally, the council approved federal grant fund allocations and amendments to community development programs to meet federal deadlines.

### Summary:
The committee heard a briefing on Seattle's readiness to attract an NBA team and discussed the Climate Pledge Arena's capability to host one. Speakers included the Seattle Sports Commission, NBA fans, arena representatives, and a retired NBA player. The committee voted to recommend that the full City Council adopt a resolution affirming Seattle's readiness for an NBA team and declaring the city a basketball city.

The committee also received an update on repairs to the 1st Avenue South Bridge from the Washington State Department of Transportation. No decisions or votes were taken on the bridge repair project.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed several zoning and development proposals, including an amendment to the Special Development District zoning rules, a request to convert parking spaces into an outdoor café at Bert's Marketplace Theatre in Eastern Market, and approval to sell city property at 4724/4728 Conner for $4,500 to be converted into a parking lot for an adjacent auto repair business.

The city approved four federal contracts totaling $440,000 to provide housing and emergency services for homeless residents through June 2018. The Salvation Army will operate an emergency shelter, Southwest Counseling Solutions will help residents find housing, and Cass Community Social Services will run both a warming center and a transitional housing center.

### Summary:
The Committee on Pedestrian and Traffic Safety approved multiple parking and traffic changes across Chicago. These included approving about 30 individual handicapped parking permits, removing several expired permits, and establishing new residential permit parking zones in neighborhoods including near Central Park Avenue, Wabansia Avenue, Mozart Street, and Bernard Street.

The committee also approved traffic safety improvements: creating buffer zones for residential parking on Campbell Avenue and Hoyne Avenue, adding loading and standing zones on Division Street, limiting truck weight to 5 tons on Kilbourn Avenue, and installing an all-way stop sign at 54th Street and Linder Avenue.

### Summary:
The council approved tax exemptions for four housing properties under the Private Housing Finance Law. These exemptions went to a Queens property (Block 9813, Lot 5), multiple Manhattan properties in the Rosewood Portfolio (Blocks 435, 1842, 1843, and 1877), a Queens property operated by Vesta Steinway (Block 811, Lot 1), and a Bronx property called Crotona VI (Block 2983, Lots 30 and 34). All votes passed by unanimous consent.

The council also received a communication from the Mayor regarding proposed legislation to discontinue certain parkland in Ferry Point Park in the Bronx. The details of this proposal and its implications were referred to committee for further review.

### Summary:
The City Council approved a land use review of the SPARC Kips Bay development project, which involves multiple city planning applications. The vote passed with 49 council members in favor and one abstention, and the matter was referred to the Land Use Committee for further consideration.

The Council introduced several new bills and resolutions for consideration, including a proposal to require reporting on tree planting alternatives from the Parks Department, a measure to install pavement markings on city streets, and a resolution calling on New York State to authorize street cleaning camera systems to enforce parking rules. The Council also introduced a resolution condemning mass deportation threats against immigrants.

### Summary:
The City Council introduced 11 new proposals for consideration. Key measures include requiring food delivery apps to reimburse workers for vehicle crash costs, limiting how much sidewalk property owners must maintain, creating a residential parking permit system in East Elmhurst, and preventing bike share programs from charging variable prices based on demand. Additional proposals would establish ferry service to Willets Point, require public notice before major traffic changes, set price caps on for-hire vehicle leasing, and increase oversight of asbestos inspectors.

All of these proposals were referred to relevant committees for further review and discussion before any votes take place. The measures touch on issues ranging from worker protections and transportation to property maintenance and public health.

### Summary:
The Housing and Real Estate Committee approved the sale of five city-owned properties to individual neighbors through the Adjacent Neighbors Land Acquisition Program. These properties are located at 6708 S Green St, 5130 S Wood St, 7146 S Artesian Ave, 713 N Homan Ave, and 5230 S Indiana Ave, and will go to Pierre Williams, Maria Sanchez, Juan Henriquez and Seidy Diaz, Autumn Pierce, and Steve Maurice and Felicia Epting, respectively.

The committee also approved several other real estate deals, including the sale of city property at 5036-5044 S Prairie Ave for redevelopment, a community center development project at 5021 S Wabash Ave by Urban Equities Inc., and a property transfer at 1733 N Francisco Ave to Taurino and Sophia Miranda and Sara Mills. Additionally, the committee approved grant funding for a preservation project at Ebenezer Missionary Baptist Church located at 4501 S Vincennes Ave.

### Summary:
The City Council approved six property tax exemptions for housing developments across Brooklyn, Staten Island, and the Bronx under state housing finance law. These exemptions remove real property tax obligations from specific parcels to support private housing projects in those boroughs.

The Council also passed three laws affecting emergency services: one requiring the Fire Department to provide annual reports on its vehicles and equipment, another mandating reporting on emergency medical services units, and a third requiring the Fire Department to notify firefighters about protective gear that contains harmful chemicals and provide alternatives when available.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors passed several resolutions this week. They approved a resolution urging Save Mart to reconsider closing a Lucky Supermarket at 1750 Fulton Street in the Western Addition scheduled for September 2026. The board also passed resolutions commemorating La Casa de las Madres' 50th anniversary and recognizing April 2026 as American Muslim Appreciation and Awareness Month. Additionally, they supported two state assembly bills: one to allow video imaging enforcement of parking violations in transit lanes and bikeways, and another to establish a California Animal Abuse Registry.

The board scheduled two upcoming public hearings for May 2026. On May 5, supervisors will review assessment costs for blighted property repairs, and on May 19, they will review delinquent charges related to building code enforcement violations. These hearings will determine what costs property owners may owe for city-ordered inspections and repairs.
