### Summary:
The council unanimously approved the closure of two unopened alleyways on the south side of East Fifth Avenue near the CDA Restaurant. City staff had no objections to this request, and no public comments were submitted opposing the closure.

The council also unanimously approved a major mixed-use development project totaling over $100 million on approximately 70 acres near Union Road and Wilson Farm Road. The project will include 200 townhomes (priced around $300K), 250 apartments, and 140,000 square feet of retail space, along with restaurants and a gas station. The development will be built in phases starting with townhomes in early 2025, and will feature sidewalks, green spaces, and pedestrian access throughout. The approval required annexing about 4 acres from Gaston County into the city and rezoning the larger parcel, both of which the Planning Commission had unanimously recommended.

### Summary:
The city council approved several contract amendments and new spending measures. For transportation, the East Colfax BRT project contract with Triunity, Inc. was increased by $6.9 million (to $24.7 million total) and extended 9 months through September 2028. The city also approved a $6.1 million lease extension with Denver Health for the Medical Examiner's Office through 2035, and authorized $1.8 million annually for Amazon Web Services software citywide. Additionally, the city approved $730,000 to purchase a replacement asphalt paver for street work.

On health and community services, the city expanded funding for infant formula and nutrition education through the Healthy Food for Denver's Kids Initiative, approved a new Denver Health Westside Family Health Center in District 3 using bond proceeds, and authorized the Great Kids Head Start program for the 2026-2027 school year. A contract with Leon Kelly for forensic pathology services was also increased by $1 million to support the Medical Examiner's Office.

### Summary:
The council passed several zoning and licensing changes affecting residents. They approved allowing more liquor licenses on part of South Stony Island Avenue, but banned additional liquor licenses on West Morse Avenue. The 45th Precinct area in the 19th Ward was designated a Restricted Residential Zone to prevent more shared housing units and vacation rentals. The Cubs also got a one-time exception to hold a night game at Wrigley Field on July 14, 2023.

The council also approved funding for a new park development on South Western Avenue using Tax Increment Financing (TIF) funds from the Chicago Park District. The new facility will include a headquarters building, fieldhouse, multi-purpose community rooms, and outdoor sports and play areas. The council also passed various resolutions honoring local residents, including milestone birthdays, tributes to those who passed away, and recognition of first responders and community members honored at the Mayfair Salute to American Heroes.

### Summary:
The council approved several contracts and agreements, including a real estate data subscription service, property acquisition for a drainage project south of Dobbins Road, utility easements across multiple districts, and pest control services citywide. They also authorized amendments to worker compensation services and approved pet health insurance for city employees and retirees.

The council extended a ground lease agreement for Yucca Library to allow for an expansion funded by a previous bond measure. During discussion, Councilwoman Pastor proposed that if the city builds a new library on city-owned land at 19th and Montebello Avenue in the future, the current leased library building at 15th and Montebello could be repurposed for heat respite centers or other community needs. The council also approved gaming grants from the Gila River Indian Community and federal loans for affordable housing developments.

### Summary:
The council unanimously approved several items, including amendments to a development agreement with NorthPoint Development LLC for Gateway85 Industrial, authorization to demolish a house at 626 N. Modena St., and a new 25 mph speed limit on Sycamore Street plus parking and stop sign changes in Camber Woods and Nolen Farms subdivisions. The council also approved purchasing network equipment for $210,945.55 and accepted the 2023 meeting schedule.

During public comment, a resident raised concerns about age division differences between girls' and boys' youth basketball leagues in Gastonia and Belmont Parks and Recreation. The city manager reported that the city weathered Hurricane Ian well, announced that a General Obligation Bond for transportation improvements will appear on the November 8 ballot, and noted that Free Trash Week was successful with 387 tons collected. The city is actively recruiting for a Chief of Police, Deputy City Manager, and Director of Parks and Recreation.

### Summary:
The city council approved a $32 million contract with W.W. Grainger, Inc. for industrial supplies. The contract was increased by $25 million from the original amount and will run through November 2026, allowing city departments to purchase the supplies they need during this period.

Supervisor Matt Dorsey requested that several city agencies—including the Police Department, Fire Department, and Department of Public Health—conduct a needs assessment on using court-mandated drug treatment programs to address public drug use in San Francisco.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed two event petitions, both recommended for approval. The Shul Chabad Lubavitch requested permission to hold "Menorah in the D" at Cadillac Square and Campus Martius on December 27, 2016, with temporary street closures on Woodward and Cadillac Square. Jonathan Witz & Associates requested approval to host the "2017 Meridian Winter Blast" at Campus Martius Park on February 10-12, 2017, also requiring temporary street closures.

The city is pursuing several funding opportunities and accepting donations for parks and recreation. The Department of Recreation is applying for a $41,000 State Farm grant and a $9,025 National Park Service grant to support conservation and youth outdoor programs. Additionally, the city accepted three major donations: a $1.5 million synthetic turf athletic field for Chandler Park, $270,000 in new tennis courts for Chandler Park, and an art installation called "Art Forest Mural Structures" for Krainz Woods Park. The committee also approved a contract increase of $24,000 for a project compliance assistant position through March 2017.

### Summary:
The City Council passed four transportation-related laws on December 18, 2025. The first protects for-hire vehicle drivers (like Uber and Lyft drivers) from wrongful deactivation. The second establishes a pilot program for parking designated for for-hire vehicles. The third requires vegetation to be planted on new medians that separate bike lanes from car traffic. The fourth directs the Department of Transportation to study the commuter van industry.

All four measures passed with strong majorities, ranging from 40 to 43 votes in favor. The vegetation planting law received the most support with 43 votes, while the for-hire vehicle driver protection and parking pilot program each got 40 votes. The commuter van industry study also passed with 40 votes.

### Summary:
The city council approved numerous contracts and agreements affecting Phoenix's infrastructure and services. Key approvals included traffic signal equipment purchases, water main replacement planning for 2026-2030, sewer and storm drain cleaning services, and road improvements on 67th Avenue in partnership with Peoria. The council also authorized land acquisition for pedestrian improvements at Roosevelt and 28th Streets, accepted EPA brownfields funding for multiple districts, and approved an airport lease amendment at Sky Harbor International Airport.

The council also moved forward on administrative matters, including a proposed annexation of 9th Avenue and Alameda Road in District 1, forensic DNA software maintenance for police, and a license agreement extension with the Royal Palm Neighborhood. One item regarding city employee pay classifications was delayed until February 4, 2026, while a law enforcement records system agreement was withdrawn from consideration.

### Summary:
The City Council approved several zoning and development projects on November 25, 2025. This included a special permit for a property in Manhattan's Midtown Theater District and approval of the Praise Tabernacle development project in Queens at 150-20 108th Avenue, which was designated as an Urban Development Action Area to facilitate the project.

The Council also passed two new laws to improve accessibility for people with disabilities: one requiring printed city documents to follow accessibility guidelines, and another requiring the city to provide information about wheelchair repair providers. Additionally, the Mayor submitted Mitchell Katz's name for reappointment to the New York City Board of Health for Council approval.

Revenue Projections and Proposed Layoffs to City Workers Mayor Daniel Lurie addressed the Board of Supervisors regarding the finalization of the proposed annual budget, including changes made to address the deficit and the impacts of social service programs and subsidies. ### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors met on April 14, 2026, with 10 of 11 supervisors present. The main discussion focused on the city's budget deficit and proposed cuts to city workers. Supervisor Chen questioned Mayor Lurie about why the city was still planning $100 million in worker cuts, given that the deficit projection had improved by 30%, the city received a $120 million settlement from Airbnb, and there's potential for up to $300 million in new revenue from Proposition D.

The meeting also covered routine items: the board approved minutes from the previous meeting in March and excused Supervisor Fielder's absence. The meeting included the standard pledge of allegiance and acknowledgement of the Ramaytush Ohlone as the original inhabitants of San Francisco.

### Summary:
Denver City Council approved nine appointments and reappointments to various boards and commissions on April 7, 2026. The positions filled include members to the Colfax Mayfair and Federal Boulevard Business Improvement Districts, the Crime Prevention and Control Commission, the Denver Health and Hospital Authority Board, the Denver Urban Renewal Authority Board, and the Mile-High Regional Emergency Medical & Trauma Advisory Council. All appointments were approved by consent, meaning they passed without individual discussion or debate.

These are routine administrative decisions that fill vacant or expiring seats on city boards. The appointed individuals will serve terms ranging from two to five years, depending on the board. While these positions don't directly affect your taxes or immediate city services, board members help shape policy in areas like public health, emergency response, and neighborhood business development.

### Summary:
The Phoenix City Council approved eight new liquor licenses for various businesses including restaurants, breweries, and a meat market, while rejecting one application from Hatcher Beer and Smoke Shop in District 3 due to neighborhood protests and unresolved building and zoning issues. The council also approved two special event liquor licenses for St. Francis Xavier Roman Catholic Parish and CASA Academy.

In other business, the council adopted a new campaign finance reporting requirement ordinance, approved a software licensing contract for community communication and call triage services, and settled two claims against the city (Gomez and Rojas cases). A rezoning request for the northwest corner of 24th Street and Missouri Avenue was postponed to January 7, 2026, and a data center waiver request was pushed to December 17.

### Summary:
The city approved two new housing projects. The Helen Drake Village project will add 88 affordable housing units for seniors in District 5, with construction starting in December. A separate multifamily project will bring 132 new units to the urban core, with many reserved for low- to moderate-income families. Both projects are partnerships between the city and private developers, using financing tools to create affordable housing near jobs, schools, and transit.

The council also approved several infrastructure and airport-related purchases, including equipment upgrades at Phoenix Sky Harbor and Deer Valley airports, water treatment plant rehabilitation work, and fire department dispatch transition services. Two zoning changes were approved: one near Interstate 17 and Sonoran Desert Drive, and another for the Laveen Towne Center development. A citywide middle housing code amendment was postponed to November 19, though city staff warned the city must comply with state housing law by January 1, 2026, or risk losing local zoning control.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors discussed several items affecting San Francisco residents. Most significantly, they extended the Planning Commission's review period by 90 days for a new rule that would make it easier to build 100% affordable housing by removing some standard building requirements like ground-floor commercial spaces. They also supported two state bills: one that would prevent vehicle registration renewal for people with unpaid illegal dumping fines, and another establishing a pilot program requiring speed-limiting devices for drivers convicted of severe speeding violations.

The board also initiated landmark designation reviews for three historic buildings—the Century Club of California, the Religious School for the Congregation Emanu-El/Grabhorn Press Building, and the Inverness Garage—which would protect them from demolition or major changes. Additionally, they approved commemorative recognitions including naming the 300 block of Eddy Street after fallen Police Officer Elia Lewin-Tankel and designating March 28, 2026, as John and Ina Dearman Day to honor their civil rights work.

### Summary:
Councilwoman Hernandez proposed changes to Phoenix's 2025-26 budget, including redirecting $7 million from Police Department overtime to fund emergency housing vouchers ($4.6 million), parks ($2 million), libraries ($249,000), and arts and culture ($175,000). She also advocated for protecting city programs and expressed concerns about police overtime spending, while noting $109 million is set aside to address a 2027 budget crisis.

The council heard from dozens of residents during public comment. Those in support included police and firefighter representatives, while those in opposition or requesting changes asked for priorities like a heat relief program for Maryvale, tenant protections during evictions, affordable housing investments, and more funding for community services and green spaces.

### Summary:
The committee reviewed several complaints about dangerous structures and illegal dumping at four different locations (Coyle, Ward, Memorial, and W. Chicago/Rutland) that residents had raised at a community meeting. The committee also discussed a proposed change to police secondary employment rules that would let the Police Chief set pay rates for officers doing outside work, subject to City Council approval.

The committee approved contracts including $324,674.94 for radios and chargers for the city's transit department (DDOT), funded 80% federally and 20% by the state; $19,800 for emergency demolition of a building at 544 Jefferson; $60,000 for an airport lease agreement; and $26,500 for emergency demolition of a building at 4049 W. Vernor. The committee also discussed parking violation fee reductions, sidewalk repair improvements, and a proposal to allow individual sales of prepackaged ice and cups.

### Summary:
The Committee on Pedestrian and Traffic Safety recommended approval of 14 handicapped parking permits and one permit removal. The committee also approved residential permit parking changes in three neighborhoods on the South and West Sides.

The committee recommended approval of multiple traffic and safety changes across Chicago, including new stop signs at various intersections, a 20 mph speed limit on N Clarendon Ave, weight restrictions on eight South Side streets, removal of some parking time limits, and changes to loading zones and truck parking restrictions at several locations.

### Summary:
The Board of Supervisors passed Resolution 069-26 with 11 votes in favor. The board also introduced several new ordinances for committee review, including changes to downtown activation zones in alleys and streets near Mission Street, Minna Alley, Natoma Street, Second Street, and Market Street near Yerba Buena Lane to allow for more public gathering spaces.

The supervisors also proposed updates to the sidewalk flower stand permit program, requiring operators to work at least 35 hours per week, be present at their stands at least half the time, and dedicate 75% of space to flowers and plants. Permits would no longer be transferable through sale, inheritance, or lease. Additionally, a separate ordinance would modify housing development rules to exclude certain industrial areas and sites far from transit from state requirements to allow dense residential development, while allowing greater density in areas near transit stops.

### Summary:
The city is selling six vacant or underutilized properties to private buyers at low prices. The sales include a parking lot for a funeral parlor on Seven Mile ($10,000), parking space for a cabaret on Livernois ($5,600), greenspace near an industrial facility on Meldrum and Beaufait ($17,347), parking adjacent to a facility on Commonwealth ($16,206), greenspace in a residential area on Dwyer ($2,085), and a single-family home on Flanders ($3,600).

Most of these uses are already allowed under current zoning rules. However, the Commonwealth Avenue property requires the buyer to obtain special approval or zoning changes before the sale can be completed, since parking isn't currently permitted there. All proposals were referred to the Planning and Economic Development Committee on November 3, 2016.

### Summary:
The council approved adding a contract award to the agenda: $511,327 to GovDirect, Inc. for the purchase of mobile laptop computers. The meeting also included recognitions of city employees and volunteers, including a 50-year service award to Sandra Merrell and a 21-year award to Patti Plaksin for her work as a Stormwater Commissioner. Two summer interns were introduced to the city.

During public comment, more than a dozen residents spoke in support of Faith Hope & Love Ministries, a local organization that helps people in need. They asked the city to partner with the ministry to address homelessness in Gastonia. The council also heard details about the Fourth of July celebration happening July 4 downtown, which will feature live music, over 50 vendor booths, food trucks, and a fireworks show starting at 9:30 p.m.

### Summary:
The City Council voted to approve a new law allowing tax liens on abandoned properties to be transferred to a land bank, which can help the city reclaim and redevelop vacant buildings. The council also approved establishing a Coney Island business improvement district, a special commercial area that can manage its own improvements and services.

The council additionally passed changes to the city's 2027 budget timeline, adjusting when various financial reports and budget documents must be submitted by the mayor, city agencies, and community boards. A separate resolution approved funding allocations to various organizations in the city budget.
